goal: Find Meta Platforms' 2026 capital expenditure guidance as originally disclosed on its Q4/full-year 2025 earnings call (late January 2026), including the exact range.
expected: $115 billion to $135 billion in 2026 capex (including principal payments on finance leases), as originally disclosed on Meta's Q4/FY2025 earnings call in late January 2026 (vs. $72.22B spent in 2025). Meta later RAISED this to $125-145 billion at Q1 2026 earnings; the original Q4 2025 figure is $115-135B. As of 2026-06-18.
answer:
## Meta Platforms' 2026 Capital Expenditure Guidance (Q4/Full-Year 2025 Earnings Call — January 28, 2026)

On its Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings call held on **January 28, 2026**, Meta Platforms disclosed the following 2026 capital expenditure guidance:

> **$115 billion to $135 billion** (including principal payments on finance leases) [1][2]

### Key Details

| Item | Figure |
|---|---|
| **2026 Capex Guidance Range** | **$115B – $135B** |
| Midpoint | ~$125B |
| 2025 Actual Capex (for comparison) | $72.22 billion |
| Year-over-Year Increase | ~60–87% |

### Context & Rationale
- The guidance covered investments in **data centers, servers, and AI infrastructure**, representing nearly **double** Meta's 2025 capex of ~$72 billion [2][3].
- Management attributed the massive step-up to increased investment supporting **Meta Superintelligence Labs** efforts and its core business [1].
- Despite the significant increase in infrastructure spending, Meta stated it expected **2026 operating income to exceed 2025 levels** [1].
- CFO **Susan Li** presented the financial outlook, framing the investment as critical to powering Meta's AI ambitions [3].

### Subsequent Revision
For reference, Meta later **raised** this guidance on its Q1 2026 earnings call (April 30, 2026) to **$125 billion–$145 billion**, citing even greater AI infrastructure needs [4].

---
*The $115B–$135B range is the figure as **originally disclosed** on the January 28, 2026 earnings call.*

Confirms Meta raised its 2026 capex guidance to $125B–$145B in April 2026, up from the original $115B–$135B disclosed in January 2026.
